inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Medic's Satchel
inventory.description: Herb rolls, boiled linen, needle and gut. Valley medicine — patient, practical, proven.

inventory.name: Healing Draught
inventory.description: Bitter green tincture from the terrace gardens. Knits small wounds and dulls big ones.

turns.summary: Adelheid arrives at Millbrook's river dock at dawn, finds thin catches and gray-green water, and is noticed by a sharp-eyed woman near the well.
turns.narration:
The Echo River smells clean this morning — or almost clean. You arrived in Millbrook just before dawn, following the valley road down from the higher terraces, your Traveler's Pack pulling at your shoulders after three days of walking. Now the village is waking around you: smoke from cook-fires, the creak of water wheels catching the current, a dog barking at nothing near the granary. You're standing at the edge of the river dock, where wooden planks extend over brown-green water and two fishermen are hauling up nets with the particular silence of people who already know what they'll find.

The catch is thin. One of them, a heavyset woman with a sun-cracked face, shakes the net once and says nothing. The other, younger, stares at the water and then away. A faint gray-green iridescence hangs on the river's surface where the light catches it — like oil, but wrong. You've been long enough in mountain valleys to know this isn't right, but you've never seen this color before.

Nearby, the village well stands in a small plaza where two lanes meet. A handful of early risers are drawing water there instead of from the river, which tells you something about local habits. A woman in a patched green coat is moving between the well and the market stalls, directing a boy who's clearly her errand-runner, her manner efficient and appraising. She notices you noticing the river, and for a moment her eyes rest on your Medic's Satchel with calm, professional interest.

You don't know anyone here yet. The village doesn't know you. But the morning already has the quiet charge of a place where something is wrong and everyone is waiting for someone to say it first.

turns.summary: Adelheid uses Echo Sense and discovers fish are avoiding the near bank; the riverbed shows an unnaturally flat, dense deposit.
turns.narration:
You press your boot heel gently against the dock's worn planks and let the ability open — a quiet thing, more like tuning your ears to a lower register than any dramatic gesture. Echo Sense costs you four energy and gives you maybe two seconds of clean information.

What comes back isn't nothing. Below the dock, the river runs at its usual early-spring push, current strong enough that you feel the planks shiver in your calves. Fish are present — more than the nets suggest — but they're hanging deep, pressed toward the opposite bank, as if the shallows under the dock are somewhere they'd rather not be. You map a dozen bodies clustered in one cold seam of the river where the bottom shelves into deeper water. In the immediate ten paces beneath you, the bottom itself is unusually flat and faintly dense — sediment, maybe, or something deposited in it. Not natural river-bottom texture.

The heavyset fisherwoman snaps her net once and mutters something you don't catch, then shoves it into a bucket. The young fisherman is still staring at a point three feet past his own reflection, the way people stare when they've already decided not to ask the question in front of them. Behind you, thirty paces up the lane, the woman in the patched green coat has stopped directing her errand-boy. She's looking at you with the direct, measuring calm of someone who catalogues strangers quickly and thoroughly.

You know two things the fishermen probably don't have language for yet: the fish aren't gone, they're avoiding something specific to this bank. And whatever is settling in the riverbed here is doing so with a flatness that silt doesn't usually manage on its own.

turns.summary: Adelheid approaches the watching woman, who introduces herself as Mara Wells — Millbrook's de facto civic manager.
turns.narration:
You turn from the water and walk the thirty paces up the lane toward her — no hesitation, no pretense of casualness. The woman in the patched green coat tracks your approach with the same measuring patience she's used since you arrived. Her errand-boy, maybe twelve, shifts from foot to foot and glances between you and her like he's waiting to see which way the weather breaks.

She speaks first, before you've quite closed the gap. "Medic's Satchel, Harmonic cut to the stitching on the strap, and you spent four minutes watching a river that everyone here has decided not to look at." Her voice is dry, unhurried, the kind that expects to be answered honestly. "You're not from Millbrook." It isn't a question. Her eyes drop briefly to the satchel, then back to your face.

The errand-boy takes his cue and sets down a cloth-wrapped bundle near the well's stone lip — something heavy, carefully packed — then steps back to a respectful distance. The woman extends a hand. Her grip, when she offers it, is firm and slightly rough, a worker's hand on a planner's frame. "Mara Wells. I manage what passes for civic order in this village, which is less impressive than it sounds." A faint dry smile. "And you are?"

turns.summary: Adelheid introduces herself honestly to Mara Wells, who offers a vague errand in return.
turns.narration:
You take her hand — a firm grip, brief and direct. "Adelheid. From Switzerland originally, though I've been living in the mountains above Jeonju for the past few years." You let that settle, then: "Herbalist, mostly. Medic when someone needs it. I came down to learn what grows here before the season turns." A small pause, honest rather than dramatic. "The water caught my eye because I've been watching rivers since I was seven, and that color doesn't belong there."

Mara Wells releases your hand and doesn't fill the silence immediately. She's reassessing — not suspiciously, more the way a careful person updates a working estimate. Behind you, the dock planks creak as the heavyset fisherwoman shifts her weight without approaching. The errand-boy glances at the cloth-wrapped bundle on the well's stone lip, then back to Mara, then carefully away.

"Adelheid." She says it once, like she's filing it. "Switzerland is a long walk." Not quite a question. Her eyes drop briefly to your Medic's Satchel, then lift again. "The plants here will be new to you, then. Some of what grows on the lower terraces has no name in any catalog I've seen — it's changed since the Transition, or it was always here and nobody wrote it down." She says it plainly, not as a gift, more as a fact she's deciding whether to use. "I have an errand that would take someone with your eyes about an hour. The woman who used to do it left Millbrook three weeks ago and I haven't found a good replacement."

She doesn't look at the bundle yet. The morning light is strengthening behind her, first gold of early spring cutting along the lane, catching the faint iridescence on the river surface thirty paces down.

turns.summary: Mara confirms the errand to Piotr and quietly asks Adelheid to observe the uncatalogued plants on the east terrace — with permission to take cuttings.
turns.narration:
You shift the bundle's weight to get a better feel for what's inside — dense and settled, the way packed roots or sealed clay sits differently from cloth or grain. Mara watches you find the balance without comment, which itself tells you something: she's used to people who handle things.

"Piotr," you repeat. "Terrace three, east side, behind the mill. And the plant note stays between him and me." You meet her eyes to let her know you heard the second part clearly. She gives a single nod — not warm exactly, but satisfied. The kind of acknowledgment that passes between people who've decided, provisionally, to trust each other.

She doesn't let you go quite yet. Her voice drops enough that the fishwives at the stall twenty paces down the lane won't catch it. "While you're up there — the east terrace borders the untended stretch. You'll see it. Plants coming up where nothing was catalogued before the Transition. I've been watching them for two seasons." A beat. "If something looks like it doesn't belong, you have my permission to take a cutting. Carefully." The way she says *carefully* suggests she means something more specific than 'don't damage the roots.'

She steps back then, a clean dismissal — she has a village to manage and you have an errand to run. The morning is full now, light cutting down the lane between the stall awnings, and the path up toward the mill terraces begins just past the market's far end.

turns.summary: Bundle delivered to Piotr on Terrace Three east; Adelheid observes the uncatalogued silver-stemmed plants at the terrace's edge.
turns.narration:
The lane behind the mill is quieter than the well plaza — fewer stalls, more mud, the smell of turned earth and water-wheel damp. You shift the bundle against your hip and follow the path upward as it narrows between low stone walls. Early-spring growth pushes through the gaps: chickweed, dead nettle, the occasional volunteer barley. Nothing unusual yet, but you're paying attention the way Mara's word 'carefully' lodged in your mind like a splinter.

Terrace Three announces itself as a wider shelf of land cut into the slope, bordered by a low retaining wall on the downhill side. Most of it is tidy — rows of early brassicas under light mulch, irrigation channels cut clean. The east edge is different. You notice it before you're even looking for it: the growth there is denser and lower to the ground, spreading in a way that doesn't follow the terrace's layout. Leaves with a faint silvery underside. Stems that branch in pairs at every node, very regular, almost too regular. No catalog entry surfaces in your memory to match the shape. You make a note to come back.

Piotr is easy to find — a compact man in his fifties, kneeling at the far end of the terrace patching an irrigation channel with river clay, gray-streaked hair pushed back under a faded cloth cap. He looks up when he hears your footsteps and his gaze goes straight to the bundle before it reaches your face. He straightens, wipes his hands on his thighs, and comes to meet you. "From Mara," you say, which is all you need to say. He takes the bundle in both hands with a care that tells you the clay jars inside matter to him, sets it on the wall, and begins working the cord loose with practiced fingers — then stops, glances at you. "There's a note?" "Inside." "Good." He doesn't open it in front of you.

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: The lamp swings once, twice, and you drop to one knee at the dock's edge without making a show of it
Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar studies the iridescent water; the dock-woman breaks her silence and addresses him directly.
Adelheid examines the silver-stemmed plants at the east edge — and feels a hum inside the stem.
turns.narration:
Piotr's hands still on the cord. He glances at you once, then at the bundle, then back — the practiced economy of a man who waits for permission he's already decided to give himself. Whatever Mara put in that note, he considers it his business the moment you're gone. You let him have that. The bundle is delivered; your task now is standing fifteen paces east, where the terrace stops being Piotr's and starts being something else's.

The silver-stemmed growth comes up to mid-thigh at its densest point, spilling past the terrace edge in a way that has nothing to do with how Piotr laid his irrigation lines. You crouch at the boundary and look without touching first. The leaves are broad, dark green on top — but underneath, that silver is almost metallic, catching the midday light in a way plant tissue shouldn't. The paired branching Mara mentioned is unmistakable up close: every node forks at exactly the same angle, left-right, left-right, like a notation rather than a growth. You have catalogued hundreds of herbs from two continents and studied the valley's post-Transition flora with half a dozen naturopaths. This pattern does not belong to any of them.

You open your Medic's Satchel slowly and reach for the folded linen you use for cuttings. The stems nearest the terrace edge are younger, thinner — a safer place to start than the dense interior. When your fingers close around the first stem to position your knife, you feel a faint, almost sub-audible hum move through it. Not vibration from the wind. From inside the plant. You hold very still.

turns.summary: Adelheid touches the silver-stemmed plant and feels the internal hum; Aevi resonates toward it in quiet fascination.
turns.narration:
Piotr's hands still on the cord — he glances once at you, once at the bundle, then back, the practiced economy of a man who has already decided the note is his business the moment you're gone. You let him have that. The bundle is delivered. Your task now is standing fifteen paces east, where the terrace stops being Piotr's and starts being something else's.

The silver-stemmed growth comes up to mid-thigh at its densest point, spilling past the terrace edge in a way that has nothing to do with how Piotr laid his irrigation lines. You crouch at the boundary and look before you touch. The leaves are broad, dark green above, silver beneath — that undersurface catches the midday light like hammered foil. The branching is what stops you: every node forks in paired symmetry, left and right, exact, the way living things almost never bother to be. It spreads outside the terrace grid as if the grid simply isn't a fact it has registered.

You press two fingers to the nearest stem. The hum arrives immediately — not through the air, not a surface tremor, but from inside the tissue itself, rising into your fingertips like a held note from a struck bell that hasn't quite finished sounding. Aevi drifts forward from your shoulder, motes pulling tight into a bright teal cluster just above the leaves. It doesn't speak. It hums back — a single, low frequency, barely audible — and the cluster brightens a fraction, as if listening for an answer.

Behind you, Piotr has not moved. The cord on the bundle is still half-worked. He is watching you with the stillness of a man who has stood at this terrace edge before and chosen not to cross it.

turns.summary: Echo Sense maps the silver-stemmed plants — the hum has a direction, the roots are unified, and Aevi names it: one system.
turns.narration:
You close your eyes and let the breath out slow. Four energy, conserved — a narrow beam rather than a flood. The echo goes in like a question asked quietly in a library.

What comes back is not dramatic. It is precise. The roots of these plants go deep — deeper than brassicas have any business going, deeper than Piotr's irrigation lines, deeper than the terrace's own cut-stone foundation. You count them: six, seven main root channels, each dropping at the same angle, each spaced with the same interval as the branching above. The hum you felt through your fingers has a direction. It does not rise from the soil. It travels along the roots horizontally, south-southeast, disappearing past the range of your sense like a word spoken in the next room. Something is down there that the roots are reaching toward, or reaching from.

Aevi goes very still. The motes tighten into a single bright point above the nearest leaf and hold there, not humming now — listening. After a moment, a soft voice behind your ear: "It is not one plant. It is one system." Then silence, which from Aevi counts as emphasis.

Piotr has not moved. The cord is still half-worked. He is watching your face, not the plant.

Piotr has not moved. The cord is still half-worked. He is watching your face, not the plant.

choices.label: Ask Piotr directly — does he know what's underneath, and how long the roots have been going that deep?
Take a cutting now — careful, as Mara said — and note the root angle and spacing in the linen wrap before the memory fades.
Follow the hum's direction with Echo Sense — scan south-southeast along the root line to see if it leads to something identifiable under the terrace.
Say nothing yet — sketch the root pattern and direction in your watercolors before anything shifts, then decide what to share.

Mara's previous errand-runner left Millbrook three weeks ago and hasn't been replaced
Mara considers the untamed lower-terrace plants significant enough to mention unprompted
Mara has been watching the uncatalogued east-terrace plants for two full seasons
She considers them significant enough to task a stranger with observing them on a first meeting
Her use of 'carefully' when describing cutting them implies she suspects they may react or matter in a way beyond ordinary botany
skillsObserved: information economy — shares just enough to create interest
information economy — shares just enough to create obligatio
mara-wells.statusNote: Active in Millbrook village lane; has delegated the Piotr errand and the terrace observation to Adelheid and returned to civic rounds.

piotr.name: Piotr
piotr.description: A compact man in his fifties with gray-streaked hair; kneels in his field patching irrigation channels, hands permanently clay-stained. Speaks minimally. Recognized the bundle before he recognized Adelheid.
history: [d1] Received the bundle from Adelheid without questions. Confirmed the note is inside; stopped himself from opening it in front of her. Manner suggests he was expecting this delivery.
[d1] Watched Adelheid move toward the silver-stemmed plants. Did not stop her. Did not look away. Holding the bundle still.
[d1] Watched Adelheid make first contact with the silver-stemmed plants. Has not moved, has not spoken, has not crossed toward the east edge. His stillness reads as deliberate familiari
[d1] Watched Adelheid's face during the Echo Sense pass — not the plant, her face. Still has not spoken. Cord still half-worked.
knownFacts: Was expecting a delivery from Mara — recognized the bundle immediately
Chose not to open the private plant note in Adelheid's presence
Works Terrace Three east himself; knows this land well
He watches the east-edge plants without approaching them — possible familiarity or wariness
He watches the east-edge plants without approaching — pattern suggests he has made a conscious choice not to touch them
He watches Adelheid's expression during her ability use — suggesting he is waiting to see what she finds, not whether she uses an ability
piotr.statusNote: On Terrace Three east, holding Mara's bundle, watching Adelheid's face after the Echo Sense pass — has not spoken, has not moved.

notes: [d1] The path to the mill terraces begins at the far end of the market lane. Terrace Three east borders an untended stretch of uncatalogued plants that Mara Wells has been monitoring for two seasons.

[d1] Terrace Three east has an untended east edge colonized by an uncatalogued silver-leafed plant with paired branching at every node — spreading outside the terrace grid. First observed Day 1 midday.
[d1] The silver-stemmed east-edge plants hum internally when touched — confirmed Day 1 midday by Adelheid on first direct contact.
[d1] Aevi responded to the silver-stemmed plants with a low resonant hum and brightened — the motes pulled tight and close above the leaves, as if listening for an answer from the plant tissue.
[d1] Echo Sense confirmed: the east-edge plant roots are unified and deep, angled south-southeast — the hum has a direction and a system behind it.

terrace-three-east.name: Terrace Three East
terrace-three-east.note: Root system confirmed unified — hum travels south-southeast along shared roots, depth exceeds terrace foundation. Aevi n

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

Word reaches you: The six-fold subsurface pulse confirmed reaching Millbrook's drinking wells — the pre-Transition system's reach now encompasses the village water supply, not merely the river and irrigation channels (ne
Word reaches you: Pell granted permission for Sela to testify at Thursday's elder meeting — on condition that Pell herself stands at the door and holds the right to call it off. (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: A second anonymous annotation appeared on the Edge District assembly notice board — 'ask the river' — added in fresh ink on filtration run night. (near radiant plateau edge)

bio.residence: a mountain village in South Korea
bio.hobbies: watercolor painting, tea with the villagers
bio.motivation: she had to move to South Korea due to her parents' occupation
bio.story: Adelheid was always learning about plants in Switzerland and was known in her village for clever remedies and even special cough drops. She has been learning the new plants in South Korea and has studied with other naturopaths and herbalists and is creating her own now.

echo-river-contamination.label: Echo River Contamination

mara-wells.label: Mara Wells
facts: [d1] Mara Wells manages civic order in Millbrook — she spotted Adelheid's Medic's Satchel and Harmonic stitching immediately and initiated contact, suggesting she was already looking for someone useful.

valley-plants.label: Valley Flora — Post-Transition
facts: [d1] Mara Wells notes that plants on the lower terraces around Millbrook have changed since the Transition — some have no name in any catalog. Either they evolved, or they were always here unrecorded. Pote

uncatalogued-terrace-plants.label: Uncatalogued Terrace Plants
facts: [d1] Plants have been growing on Millbrook's east lower terrace for at least two seasons that appear in no catalog before the Transition. Mara Wells has been watching them and considers them significant —

[d1] First direct observation on Terrace Three east: silver-undersided leaves, stems branching in paired nodes with unusual regularity — pattern feels almost systematic. Spreads outside the terrace layout.

piotr.label: Piotr
facts: [d1] Compact farmer, Terrace Three east. Was expecting Mara's bundle. Sparing with words. Trusted enough by Mara to receive a private plant note.

establishedFacts.text: The silver-stemmed uncatalogued plants on Terrace Three east emit a faint internal hum when touched — not wind, not vibration, but originating from within the plant tissue.

The silver-stemmed east-edge plants share a single unified root system that descends far deeper than the terrace cut — and the internal hum travels south-southeast along the roots, past the range of E
